rationale: Cost-only drivers favor the fastest path off the legacy license; lift-and-shift realizes savings soonest.
Performance gains need set-based refactor or re-architecture; mechanical conversion preserves slow patterns.
AI/Cortex value depends on a modern model + semantic layer, strongly favoring re-architect.
M&A/consolidation needs a unified target model; re-architecture is the natural fit.
Regulatory drivers need governance baked in; refactor or rewrite let you embed masking/lineage at build time.
Hard deadlines penalize rewrite and favor automated conversion.
High procedural complexity (cursors, dynamic SQL) carries badly through mechanical conversion; refactor/rewrite pay off.
Simple codebases convert cleanly; lift-and-shift is low risk and sufficient.
Mature Snowflake teams can absorb a rewrite and exploit native features.
Low-maturity teams should not attempt a re-architecture as their first Snowflake project.
Real-time/CDC needs Snowpipe Streaming + new pipelines, not a like-for-like batch port.
Explicit appetite for modernization/greenfield strongly favors re-architecture.
Explicit lift-and-shift/minimal-change intent favors automated conversion.
SSIS does not run on Snowflake; refactor to ELT/dbt is the mainstream path, but a quick retarget is possible.
Informatica can retarget to Snowflake (replatform) or be rebuilt in dbt; refactor balances both.
Existing dbt usage signals readiness for ELT-native re-architecture.
Teradata cost-reduction migrations benefit from set-based refactor; BTEQ rarely ports cleanly as-is.
Heavy PL/SQL (packages) must be rewritten regardless; refactor captures that without full re-architecture.
